title: Reading and Use of English - Part 8
description: Read the four opinions on Artificial Intelligence. For questions 47 – 56, choose from the experts (A – D).

texts.A:
### The Skeptic (Dr. Aris)
'We are prone to anthropomorphizing code,' says Dr. Aris. [47]He argues that just because a machine can mimic empathy, it doesn't mean it's actually experiencing it.[/47] [51]His main concern is the 'black box' problem—we are building systems so complex that even their creators don't fully understand how they reach certain conclusions.[/51] [54]This lack of transparency makes it impossible to hold anyone accountable when things go wrong.[/54] He suggests we should stop trying to build 'gods' and focus on specialized tools that we can control.
texts.B:
### The Visionary (Sarah Chen)
Sarah Chen sees AI as the ultimate partner for human creativity. [48]She points out that throughout history, every new tool—from the camera to the synthesizer—was initially seen as a threat to 'real' art.[/48] [52]AI will handle the tedious, repetitive parts of the creative process, allowing us to focus on pure imagination.[/52] [50]She mentions that AI has already helped scientists solve biological puzzles, like protein folding, that would have taken humans decades.[/50] For her, AI is not a replacement for humanity, but an extension of it.
texts.C:
### The Ethicist (James Miller)
James Miller is focused on the immediate societal impacts. [49]He warns that the bias already present in our society is being baked into these algorithms, often in subtle, invisible ways.[/49] [53]If an AI is trained on historical data that is prejudiced, it will naturally produce prejudiced outcomes in hiring or law enforcement.[/53] [55]He is less worried about a 'killer robot' future and more about the slow erosion of human agency[/55] as we outsource our decision-making to machines that prioritize efficiency over fairness.
texts.D:
### The Tech-Executive (Kevin Vane)
Kevin Vane is all about the bottom line. [56]He admits that significant job displacement is inevitable, but argues that new, currently unimaginable roles will emerge.[/56] [48]He compares the current transition to the Industrial Revolution, noting that while it was painful, it ultimately raised living standards.[/48] [51]His priority is speed; he believes that the first countries to fully integrate AI into their infrastructure will dominate the global economy for the next century.[/51] [50]He also highlights AI's role in personalizing education, tailoring lessons to each student's specific pace.[/50]

questions.question: warn against assuming that a machine has human-like feelings?
questions.explanation: Dr. Aris argues that mimicking empathy isn't experiencing it.

questions.question: draw a historical parallel to justify current technological shifts?
questions.explanation: Sarah Chen (B) mentions cameras/synthesizers; Kevin Vane (D) mentions the Industrial Revolution.

questions.question: mention that existing human prejudices are being reinforced by technology?
questions.explanation: James Miller warns bias is being 'baked into' algorithms.

questions.question: mention a specific scientific or educational breakthrough enabled by AI?
questions.explanation: Sarah Chen (B) mentions protein folding; Kevin Vane (D) mentions personalized education.

questions.question: express a desire for more control or speed in the development of these systems?
questions.explanation: Dr. Aris (A) wants tools we can control; Kevin Vane (D) prioritizes speed to dominate the economy.

questions.question: suggest that AI will free people from the more boring aspects of their work?
questions.explanation: Sarah Chen says AI will handle 'tedious, repetitive parts' of the process.

questions.question: point out that the quality of AI output depends on the quality of its training data?
questions.explanation: James Miller says if trained on prejudiced data, it produces prejudiced outcomes.

questions.question: argue that the complexity of AI makes it difficult to assign responsibility for errors?
questions.explanation: Dr. Aris says the lack of transparency makes it impossible to hold anyone accountable.

questions.question: state that their primary concern is the loss of individual decision-making power?
questions.explanation: James Miller worries about the 'erosion of human agency'.

questions.question: acknowledge the negative impact on the job market while remaining optimistic about the future?
questions.explanation: Kevin Vane admits job displacement is inevitable but new roles will emerge.
